content:
# Matthew Chapter 25 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_025_page_001.jpg, MAT_025_page_002.jpg

### The Parable of the Ten Bridesmaids

1. "The kingdom of heaven will be like ten bridesmaids who took their lamps and went out to meet the groom.

2. Five of them were foolish and five were wise.

3. The foolish ones brought their lamps but forgot to bring extra oil.

4. The wise ones brought along flasks of oil with their lamps.

5. When the groom was delayed, they all got drowsy and fell asleep.

6. Then at midnight, a shout rang out: 'The groom is here! Come out to meet him!'

7. All the bridesmaids jumped up and got their lamps ready.

8. The foolish ones said to the wise, 'Give us some of your oil — our lamps are going out!'

9. But the wise ones replied, 'There won't be enough for all of us. Go to the shop and buy your own.'

10. While they were gone to buy oil, the groom arrived. Those who were ready went in with him to the wedding banquet, and the door was locked.

11. Later, the other bridesmaids came and called out, 'Sir! Sir! Open the door for us!'

12. But he replied, 'I tell you the truth, I don't know you.'

13. So stay alert! You don't know the day or the hour.

### The Parable of the Talents

14. "Again, the kingdom of heaven is like a man going on a long trip. He called his servants together and put them in charge of his money.

15. He gave five bags of gold to one, two bags to another, and one bag to a third — dividing it according to their abilities. Then he left.

16. The servant with five bags immediately went to work investing the money and earned five more.

17. The servant with two bags did the same and earned two more.

18. But the servant who received one bag dug a hole in the ground and buried his master's money.

19. After a long time, the master returned and settled accounts with them.

20. The servant who had received five bags came and presented ten. 'Master,' he said, 'you gave me five bags of gold. Look, I've earned five more.'

21. The master replied, 'Excellent! You are a good and faithful servant. You've been faithful with a small amount, so I'll give you much greater responsibilities. Come and share in your master's joy!'

22. The servant with two bags also came forward. 'Master,' he said, 'you gave me two bags of gold. Look, I've earned two more.'

23. The master replied, 'Excellent! You are a good and faithful servant. You've been faithful with a small amount, so I'll give you much greater responsibilities. Come and share in your master's joy!'

24. Then the servant with one bag came and said, 'Master, I know you're a tough man. You harvest where you didn't plant and gather where you didn't scatter seed.

25. I was afraid, so I went and hid your money in the ground. Here — you have what belongs to you.'

26. But the master replied, 'You wicked, lazy servant! So you knew that I harvest where I didn't plant and gather where I didn't scatter?

27. Then you should have at least deposited my money with the bankers, so I would have received it back with interest when I returned.

28. Take the bag of gold from him and give it to the one who has ten.

29. For those who use well what they are given will receive more, and they will have an abundance. But those who are unfaithful will lose even what little they have.

30. Now throw this useless servant into the outer darkness, where there will be weeping and grinding of teeth.'

### The Sheep and the Goats

31. "When the Son of Man comes in his glory, with all the angels, he will sit on his glorious throne.

32. All the nations will be gathered before him, and he will separate them as a shepherd separates the sheep from the goats.

33. He will put the sheep on his right and the goats on his left.

34. Then the King will say to those on his right, 'Come, you who are blessed by my Father. Inherit the kingdom prepared for you since the creation of the world.

35. For I was hungry, and you fed me. I was thirsty, and you gave me a drink. I was a stranger, and you invited me into your home.

36. I was naked, and you gave me clothing. I was sick, and you cared for me. I was in prison, and you visited me.'

37. Then the righteous will reply, 'Lord, when did we ever see you hungry and feed you? Or thirsty and give you something to drink?

38. When did we see you as a stranger and invite you in? Or naked and give you clothing?

39. When did we see you sick or in prison and visit you?'

40. And the King will answer, 'I tell you the truth, whatever you did for one of the least of these brothers and sisters of mine, you did for me.'

41. Then the King will turn to those on his left and say, 'Away from me, you who are cursed, into the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels.

42. For I was hungry, and you didn't feed me. I was thirsty, and you didn't give me a drink.

43. I was a stranger, and you didn't invite me in. I was naked, and you didn't clothe me. I was sick and in prison, and you didn't visit me.'

44. Then they will reply, 'Lord, when did we ever see you hungry or thirsty or a stranger or naked or sick or in prison, and not help you?'

45. And the King will answer, 'I tell you the truth, whatever you failed to do for one of the least of these, you failed to do for me.'

46. And they will go away into eternal punishment, but the righteous will go into eternal life."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verses 1-13 — The ten bridesmaids:** The parable isn't about smart vs. stupid. It's about preparation vs. presumption. You can't borrow someone else's readiness. The locked door is devastating — the groom's "I don't know you" echoes 7:23. Relationship with Jesus isn't transferable.

**Verse 15 — "talents":** A talent was a massive sum — roughly 20 years' wages. The English word "talent" (meaning ability) comes directly from this parable, one of the Bible's most lasting linguistic contributions. But the original point isn't about developing your skills — it's about faithfully stewarding what God entrusts to you while waiting for his return.

**Verses 24-25 — The fearful servant:** The third servant's excuse — "I knew you were harsh, so I was afraid" — reveals a distorted image of God. He sees the master as a demanding tyrant to be feared, not a generous lord to be served. His theology produced paralysis. Fear of failure is not faithfulness.

**Verses 31-46 — The Sheep and the Goats:** The most stunning judgment scene in the Bible. The criterion isn't doctrinal purity, religious performance, or prophetic gifts — it's whether you fed the hungry, welcomed the stranger, and visited the prisoner. And the twist: Jesus identifies himself with "the least of these." Every act of mercy toward a suffering person is an act of service to Christ. Every act of neglect is neglect of Christ. Both groups are shocked — neither the sheep nor the goats realized they were encountering Jesus.

**Verse 46 — "eternal punishment" / "eternal life":** The same Greek word (αἰώνιον) modifies both. Whatever "eternal" means for life, it means for punishment. Jesus doesn't soften this symmetry.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
